topic: The influence of Neoplatonism on the iconography of Renaissance Florence, focusing on Botticelli's mythological canvases and the intellectual circle of Lorenzo de' Medici.
How the Hanseatic League governed long-distance trade across the Baltic and North Seas, from the founding of Lübeck to the decline of the Kontor network.
Moral philosophy in Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov — the Grand Inquisitor parable, the problem of evil, and the tension between faith and reason.
The collapse of the Roman Republic: from the Gracchi reforms through Sulla's dictatorship to Caesar's crossing of the Rubicon.
Heidegger's concept of Dasein in Being and Time and its reception in mid-century existential psychiatry, with reference to Ludwig Binswanger's Daseinsanalyse.
From Vulgar Latin to the Romance languages: phonological shifts, the simplification of the case system, and the role of the Carolingian Renaissance in standardizing Old French.
The Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood: painting against the Royal Academy, medievalist aesthetics, and the poetry of Keats and Tennyson as visual subject matter.
The Congress of Vienna (1814–15): Metternich's balance of power, Talleyrand's diplomatic rehabilitation of France, and the conservative order that shaped nineteenth-century Europe.
Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics and the modern revival of virtue ethics in the work of Alasdair MacIntyre, Philippa Foot, and Rosalind Hursthouse.
Andrei Tarkovsky's poetics of time and memory: long takes, water imagery, and the philosophical theology of Stalker and Nostalghia.
Introduction to reading Sumerian cuneiform: sign morphology, syllabic values, and guided transcription of a temple-inscription from the Ur III period in the Penn Museum collection.
The concept of the 'sublime' from Longinus to Edmund Burke to Kant: rhetorical, aesthetic, and transcendental dimensions of overwhelming experience.
Ancient Greek rhetorical traditions: the Sophists versus Plato, the canons of Aristotle's Rhetoric, and the enduring structure of classical oratory in modern argumentation.
Intellectual exchange along the Silk Road: Buddhist cave art at Dunhuang, Manichaean texts at Turfan, and the transmission of papermaking to the Abbasid world.
Jane Austen's free-indirect discourse: narrative irony, the management of social distance, and the technique's debts to Samuel Richardson's epistolary method.
Simone de Beauvoir's The Second Sex: the phenomenology of embodiment, the social construction of 'woman,' andresponses from intersectional and postcolonial feminism.
Proust's In Search of Lost Time — involuntary memory, the sociology of the salons, and time as both destroyer and preserver of the self.
Baroque church architecture in Counter-Reformation Rome: Il Gesù as prototype, Borromini's fluid geometries, and Bernini's theatrical integration of sculpture and space.
The Dead Sea Scrolls and Second Temple Judaism: sectarian identity at Qumran, messianic expectations, and the reinterpretation of Habakkuk in the Pesher literature.
Viking settlement of Iceland: the Landnámabók as source, the Alþingi as proto-parliament, and the ecological consequences of deforestation on the Norse colony.
The Gothic novel and the architectural imagination: Walpole's Otranto, Radcliffe's picturesque ruins, and the haunted house as psychic space in Poe and Shelley.
The development of English common law from Magna Carta to the Glorious Revolution: the evolution of habeas corpus, parliament's legislative supremacy, and Coke's Institutes.
Ancient Egyptian funerary practices: mummification techniques, the Book of the Dead, and the theology of Ma'at from the Old Kingdom through the Ptolemaic era.
Kierkegaard's three stages of existence — aesthetic, ethical, and religious — as elaborated in Either/Or and Fear and Trembling, with responses from contemporary existential psychotherapy.
Historiographical debates on the French Revolution: Marxist orthodoxy (Lefebvre, Soboul) versus revisionism (Furet, Schama) and the linguistic turn of the 1990s.
Ovid's Metamorphoses in Renaissance painting: Titian's Diana and Actaeon, Correggio's Jupiter and Io, and the humanist practice of painting 'poesie.'
The Library of Alexandria: Hellenistic patronage, the editorial standardization of Homer, and the limits of the 'destruction' narrative in modern popular history.
Ingmar Bergman's The Seventh Seal as medieval morality play: the Danse Macabre tradition, existential dread, and the knight's confession to Death on the chapel wall.
Close-reading methodology for Victorian poetry: Tennyson's 'Ulysses,' the dramatic monologue form, and the mechanics of enjambment, caesura, and iambic variation.
Thucydides' History of the Peloponnesian War — the Melian Dialogue as realism versus idealism, the plague narrative, and the historian's method of constructed speeches.
Japanese woodblock prints (ukiyo-e) and their catalytic influence on French Impressionism: Monet's Giverny bridge, Van Gogh's reinventions of Hiroshige, and Degas' asymmetrical compositions.
Bread and wine as ritual symbols across the ancient Mediterranean: Eleusinian mysteries, Dionysian cult, early Christian Eucharist, and comparative phenomenology of sacred meals.
Nominalism versus realism in medieval philosophy: Abelard's conceptualism, Ockham's razor, and the conciliar debates at Constance and Basel over universals and ecclesial authority.
The translation movement in Abbasid Baghdad (8th–10th centuries): the Bayt al-Hikma, Hunayn ibn Ishaq's Galen translations, and the preservation of Greek philosophical medicine.
Wittgenstein's language games and the ordinary-language turn: the Tractatus picture theory, the Philosophical Investigations rule-following paradox, and Stanley Cavell's elaboration.
Political allegory in George Orwell's essays: 'Shooting an Elephant,' 'Politics and the English Language,' and the tension between democratic socialism and anti-totalitarian critique.
The philosophical schools of Hellenistic Athens: Epicurean ataraxia, Stoic logos, and Pyrrhonian skepticism as responses to the post-Aristotelian loss of metaphysical certainty.
The cartographic imagination in early modern exploration narratives: Waldseemüller's 1507 map, the Cantino planisphere, and the rhetorical construction of 'terra incognita.'
The concept of mimesis from Plato's Republic to Erich Auerbach's Mimesis: rival ontologies of representation, the prohibition of poetry, and the realist novel as modern answer.
Monastic scriptoria and the preservation of classical texts: Cassiodorus at Vivarium, the Rule of St. Benedict on lectio divina, and the copying of Cicero in Carolingian scriptoria.
The concept of 'face' (gesichtsverlust, mianzi) and honor cultures in early modern Europe and Qing China: dueling codes, ritual propriety, and the anthropology of shame versus guilt.
Hannah Arendt's The Human Condition: the vita activa, the rise of the 'social,' and the banality of evil thesis in relation to the Eichmann trial coverage.
